title: Cat Shifting Limp: Shifting Leg Lameness Guide for Owners
meta_desc: Is your cat's limp moving from leg to leg? Learn about shifting leg lameness, potential causes, and when to seek veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Shifting leg lameness in cats often indicates a systemic health issue and requires a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours. Capturing video of the limp at home is essential for an accurate diagnosis, as cats often hide symptoms at the clinic.

What is shifting leg lameness in cats?

When a cat appears to be limping on different legs at different times, it is known as shifting leg lameness. Unlike a simple sprain or break which affects a single limb, this condition often indicates a systemic issue affecting the entire body. It means the inflammation or discomfort is moving between multiple joints, making it difficult to pinpoint a single source of pain without a professional exam.

How urgent is it if my cat is limping on different legs?

  • Treat a shifting limp as a medium-priority concern that requires a veterinary evaluation within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Avoid waiting for the condition to resolve on its own, as it is rarely a self-limiting issue.
  • Seek prompt care because this symptom is frequently linked to infectious diseases or immune-mediated conditions that require specific medical intervention.

How can photos and videos of my cat help the veterinarian?

  • Record a short video of your cat walking at home to provide objective evidence of the gait abnormality.
  • Document the symptoms on camera since cats often hide signs of pain once they feel the adrenaline of being at a veterinary clinic.
  • Take clear photos of your cat's paws or joints to help the vet identify subtle swelling or redness that may be difficult to see during a physical exam.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Lameness in cats can manifest as a shifting leg lameness, characterized by a gait abnormality that may or may not be distinctly recognizable. Pain-related lameness can be classified as weight-bearing (supporting leg) or nonweight-bearing (swinging leg) lameness, though it often presents as a combination of both. Clinical assessment involves palpating the limb from distal to proximal, noting any swelling, pain, instability, crepitation, reduced range of motion, or muscle atrophy. Serial examinations before and after exercise may be necessary for subtle cases, and sedation may be required for fractious animals to facilitate palpation, radiography, and arthrocentesis. Diagnostic imaging, including radiography, ultrasonography, CT, and MRI, can also aid in determining the underlying cause of lameness.

Chapter: Neurology, Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1096)

faqs.question: What are common causes of a shifting limp in cats?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include Feline Calicivirus (often called 'limping syndrome'), immune-mediated polyarthritis, and certain bacterial infections or tick-borne diseases.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat aspirin for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human pain relief medications like aspirin, ibuprofen, or Tylenol, as these are extremely toxic and can be fatal to felines.

faqs.question: Should I wait for the limp to go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Since a shifting limp often points to an underlying infection or an overactive immune system, it is unlikely to resolve without medical intervention and may worsen without treatment.
